Much feeling was manifested at
the meeting of the chamber OF
commerce at noon today at the Sill
yer Grill. Judge ]. G. Moore at
tackled the action of the Fort
Wright committee in its recon-
mendation that congress pass bill
authorizing the war department to
state where the route of the Port
land & Seattle railroad should
cross the military reservation. This
resolution has already been for
warded to Washington, D. c., and
Judge Moore said too much haste
was used. He then read suplem-
tary resolution in which the presi-
dent and secretary of war are ask
ed to have the war department
send a government engineer and
commission here to pass upon and
select the route to be used in cross
ing the fort. Judge Moore's reso,
luton provoked much oratory and
opposition. ne queStiOll vi 10y III 3
the restitution on the table was car
ried by vote of IA to s.


Those who opposed Judge Moore
claimed that the members of the
chamber of commerce would make
themselves appear "chumps" to the
congressman and president, in that
after having sent one resolution
made in a meeting at which the
railroads were represented, they
should take further action when the
railroad representatives were alb
sent. 1t was also consideed that
any action taken at this meeting
would be used by the railroads as
a loop hole through which they
might escape and repudiate their
agreement for grade separation OF
the present line of the Northern
Pacific. AS individuals only the
members favored signing Judge
Moore's resolution.
